nativeRange.habitat: Coastal and lagoon reefs; juveniles tend to inhabit shallow, protected inshore areas while adults generally prefer reef zones with heavier coral growth, at depths typically ranging from 1 to 40 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on sponges, tunicates, algae, and small invertebrates in the wild. In captivity, generally accepts a varied diet of marine algae, spirulina, high-quality angelfish preparations, and meaty foods such as mysis shrimp and brine shrimp; feeding 2–3 times daily is commonly advised.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 3140x2355. Tack-sharp adult in-water shot; olive-yellow body with fine blue speckling, brilliant electric-blue fin margins, blue-spotted tail and labyrinthine face lines all clearly shown on a vibrant live individual.
Rickard Zerpe; 3878x2909. Sharp three-quarter frontal angle of an adult; strong greenish body, blue-purple fin outlines and labyrinthine facial pattern; different angle for variety.
Kevin Lino, NOAA/NMFS/PIFSC/ESD; 3039x2173. Sharp side profile of a distinct adult individual; vibrant sapphire-blue fin outlines and speckled greenish-yellow flanks; public domain source.

summary: The Koran Angelfish is a large Indo-Pacific species known for its remarkable juvenile-to-adult color transformation and its need for a spacious aquarium. It is generally considered suitable for intermediate keepers in a fish-only or fish-only-with-live-rock setup.
careNotes:
The Koran Angelfish (Pomacanthus semicirculatus) undergoes one of the most dramatic color changes seen in marine fish kept by hobbyists. Juveniles display dark blue coloring with bright white and electric-blue semicircular stripes, while adults gradually transition to a greenish-yellow body with blue speckling, sapphire-blue fin outlines, and a fine labyrinthine pattern across the flanks. This transformation typically takes 1–2 years to complete.

The species is not considered reef-safe. It has been known to nip at stony and soft corals, clam mantles, and other sessile invertebrates (animals that are permanently attached to the rock or substrate), and is best suited to a fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) system rather than a mixed reef aquarium.

A working temperature range of roughly 23–27.5°C is consistent with both wild-range data and hobbyist practice. The Koran Angelfish is a long-lived species; lifespans of 10–21 years have been reported in well-maintained aquariums.

Live rock should be provided in abundance to supply hiding spots and natural algae-grazing surfaces. The species tends to be aggressive toward other large angelfish and conspecifics, particularly in smaller systems, and keeping more than one is generally not advised. It is typically more peaceful with dissimilar, non-competing tankmates.

Breeding in home aquariums has not been reliably documented. The species has been successfully spawned in controlled aquaculture research settings, but this is not achievable under typical home-aquarium conditions.
breedingNotes: Has not been reliably bred in home aquariums. Successful spawning has been reported in research aquaculture settings. Wild individuals are broadcast spawners; sexing adults visually is generally not possible.
